comment_text.sn12.21:1.2: The purpose of this discourse (and the next) seems to be to extol dependent origination as a special insight of the Buddha. But it does so artlessly, simply plonking down a series of teachings without connecting them in any way. Editors and translators have tried to smooth it over, but I translate as-is. | The ten powers (of a Realized One) are explained at MN 12:9.1, where they are followed by the four kinds of self-assurance (MN 12:22.1). The powers are found independently at AN 10.21 and AN 10.22, and are claimed by Anuruddha at SN 52.15–24. The four kinds of self-assurance are at AN 4.8. | The “divine wheel” (brahmacakka) is a synonym for the “wheel of the teaching” (dhammacakka). In Śvetāśvatara Upaniṣad 1.6, the same term refers to the circle of existence in which all things live.
comment_text.sn12.21:1.3: This is a recommended meditation (MN 10:38.4) leading to perfection (SN 12.23:1.4). In one place it exemplifies the Buddha’s teaching, but there it follows organically and is marked as quote (SN 22.78:2.2). Here it is simply pasted in after the previous passage. It does not offer an integration between the aggregates and dependent origination; they simply appear side by side.
comment_text.sn12.21:1.8: This is the abstract principle of dependent origination; see discussion at SN 12.37:2.2. Again there is no textual link with the previous passages.

root_text.sn12.21:1.2: “Dasabalasamannāgato, bhikkhave, tathāgato catūhi ca vesārajjehi samannāgato āsabhaṁ ṭhānaṁ paṭijānāti, parisāsu sīhanādaṁ nadati, brahmacakkaṁ pavatteti—
root_text.sn12.21:1.3: iti rūpaṁ iti rūpassa samudayo iti rūpassa atthaṅgamo,
root_text.sn12.21:1.4: iti vedanā iti vedanāya samudayo iti vedanāya atthaṅgamo,
root_text.sn12.21:1.5: iti saññā iti saññāya samudayo iti saññāya atthaṅgamo,
root_text.sn12.21:1.6: iti saṅkhārā iti saṅkhārānaṁ samudayo iti saṅkhārānaṁ atthaṅgamo,
root_text.sn12.21:1.7: iti viññāṇaṁ iti viññāṇassa samudayo iti viññāṇassa atthaṅgamo.
root_text.sn12.21:1.8: Iti imasmiṁ sati idaṁ hoti, imassuppādā idaṁ uppajjati.
root_text.sn12.21:1.9: Imasmiṁ asati idaṁ na hoti, imassa nirodhā idaṁ nirujjhati.

translation_text.sn12.21:1.2: “Mendicants, a Realized One has ten powers and four kinds of self-assurance. With these he claims the bull’s place, roars his lion’s roar in the assemblies, and turns the divine wheel.
translation_text.sn12.21:1.3: Such is form, such is the origin of form, such is the ending of form.
translation_text.sn12.21:1.4: Such is feeling, such is the origin of feeling, such is the disappearance of feeling.
translation_text.sn12.21:1.5: Such is perception, such is the origin of perception, such is the disappearance of perception.
translation_text.sn12.21:1.6: Such are choices, such is the origin of choices, such is the disappearance of choices.
translation_text.sn12.21:1.7: Such is consciousness, such is the origin of consciousness, such is the disappearance of consciousness.
translation_text.sn12.21:1.8: When this exists, this comes to be; due to the arising of this, this arises.
translation_text.sn12.21:1.9: When this doesn’t exist, this doesn’t come to be; due to the cessation of this, this ceases. That is:
translation_text.sn12.21:1.10: Ignorance is a requirement for choices.
translation_text.sn12.21:1.11: Choices are a requirement for consciousness. …
translation_text.sn12.21:1.12: That is how this entire mass of suffering originates.
translation_text.sn12.21:1.13: When ignorance fades away and ceases with nothing left over, choices cease.
translation_text.sn12.21:1.15: That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.”
